title: Chapter 10: The Bell
content: The mirror in the Daly City Airbnb was the kind that made everything look slightly better than reality — soft edges, warm tint, the cheap goodwill of a landlord who'd never replaced the original bathroom fixture. Maya stood in front of it at 6 AM, wearing a black turtleneck she'd chosen with bitter irony, slim pants, lips bare, mascara minimal. She looked corporate. Contained. Harmless.
"I'm excited about the opportunity to contribute to Aeterna's AV infrastructure," she said to her reflection. The words came out flat. She tried again. "I'm excited about the — no."

She pressed her palms against the sink and stared at herself. Dark hair pulled back. No jewelry except the small studs in her ears — the ones Lucy had given her junior year, tiny silver moons she never took out. The turtleneck's collar sat high against her jaw. She looked like she could work in any glass building on any campus in the Valley. She looked like nobody.
That was the point.

Behind her, Chloe sat cross-legged on the pull-out couch, laptop balanced on her knees, the blue wash of the screen painting her face in cold light. She was running background checks — again — on everyone connected to the interview: Marcus Webb, the AV department heads, the HR coordinator who'd scheduled the call. Paranoia was Chloe's love language, and right now she was fluent.

The TV was on, muted. CNBC. Aeterna's stock ticker crawled across the bottom of the screen in that endless digital march, and even without sound, the green numbers felt like a heartbeat. Maya glanced at it, then back at herself.
"I'm excited about the opportunity," she said, quieter this time, "because I believe in what Aeterna is building."
She almost laughed. Almost.
"Maya." Chloe's voice cut through. Her eyes were on the TV, not the laptop. "Unmute that."
Maya found the remote on the kitchen counter, wedged between a stack of takeout menus and a half-empty bag of grocery store coffee. She pointed it at the screen and pressed the button.

"—has officially filed its S-1 with the SEC," the anchor was saying, mid-sentence, that polished CNBC cadence that made catastrophe sound like opportunity. "The Silicon Valley unicorn is seeking a valuation of fourteen billion dollars in what could be the largest biotech IPO in history. CEO Alexander Vlad is expected to ring the opening bell at the New York Stock Exchange on November fifteenth."
The remote fell from Maya's hand. It hit the linoleum with a crack that sounded like a gunshot in the small space.
November fifteenth.
The date Lucy had tapped out in Morse code against that hospital bed frame. Dot-dash-dot. 1-5. November fifteenth.
Chloe's laptop screen went dark as she closed it slowly, deliberately, like she was closing a book she'd already read and hated the ending of.
"The IPO is the harvest," Maya said. Not a question.
"The IPO is the harvest," Chloe confirmed.

Maya picked up the remote — her hands were steady, she noted, which was worse than if they'd been shaking. Steady meant her body had moved past fear into something colder. She turned the volume up two more notches. The anchor had moved on to analyst commentary, but the ticker was still crawling. $AETN. Green. Green. Green.
Chloe opened her laptop again. Her fingers moved fast. "I'm pulling the S-1."
"You can find it that fast?"

"SEC EDGAR is public, Maya. That's the point. They want everyone to read it." Chloe's voice was tight, controlled. She scrolled, her eyes scanning left to right, left to right, a reading pattern that looked almost mechanical. "Okay. Okay, here — listen to this."

She read aloud: "Aeterna's proprietary longevity treatment requires ongoing access to a diverse biological donor pool. The company has established what it believes to be the largest ethically-sourced biometric database in the industry."
The word hung in the air between them like smoke.
Ethically-sourced.

Maya tasted something metallic at the back of her throat — adrenaline, sharp and mineral, the taste of a mouth that was preparing to bite or scream. "They're going to use the IPO money to scale," she said. "More facilities. More women. They're not hiding it — they're embedding it in the corporate language."

"It's right there," Chloe said. She turned the laptop toward Maya. The text was black on white, plain as a confession. "In an SEC filing. Legally binding. They wrote 'ethically-sourced biometric database' in a document that regulators will read and investors will underwrite and no one — no one — is going to ask what it means."
"Because it sounds like 23andMe."
"It sounds like progress." Chloe closed the laptop again. "It sounds like the future."

The room was quiet except for the TV. Some analyst was talking about Aeterna's revenue projections. Maya looked at the time on her phone. 6:17 AM. Her interview was at 10:00. Three hours and forty-three minutes until she walked into a building that harvested women and filed the paperwork in triplicate.
"Okay," Maya said. "Run it again."
Chloe didn't argue. They'd been through the plan six times already, but seven was the number that felt right. Ritual, not redundancy.

"You enter through the main lobby. Marcus Webb interviews you for the AV tech position. During the interview, you note the AV infrastructure — make, model, mount points. After the interview, they'll give you a campus tour. That's standard for Aeterna — they're proud of the building. During the tour, you identify the Sublevel B entry points. Stairwell access, elevator banks, service corridors. You do not go to Sublevel B."
"Do not go to Sublevel B," Maya repeated.

"The micro-devices." Chloe reached into the bag beside the couch and pulled out three small objects that looked like ordinary cable adapters — the kind that connected a display port to HDMI, anonymous gray plastic, forgettable. Inside each one was a tiny Wi-Fi transmitter, Chloe's design, that would piggyback on Aeterna's network and create a persistent back door. "You plant these in the AV system ports near the Sublevel B access points during the tour if you get the chance. If you don't get the chance, you get out clean and we find another way."
"Clean and simple."
"Clean and simple." Chloe paused. "Don't engage Vlad directly."
"He's in New York prepping for the IPO."
"Maya."
"I know. Don't engage Vlad directly."

Chloe handed her the sunglasses. They were matte black, fashionable in a tech-campus way, indistinguishable from the pair Maya actually owned. But the left temple arm housed a camera — low resolution, grainy, but enough to capture a building's security layout. Door positions. Camera placements. Badge reader locations.
"Test them," Chloe said.
Maya put them on. The world went slightly amber through the tint. She looked at Chloe, who held up three fingers. "Three."
"Works." She took them off and set them on the counter. "I'll put them on in the car."

Chloe nodded. Then she did something she didn't usually do. She stood up and pulled Maya into a hug — brief, fierce, her arms tight around Maya's shoulders. "You come back," Chloe said into her ear. "You come back and we figure out the rest together."
"I'm coming back."
Chloe let go. "Your phone's off?"
"Will be. After I leave."
"I'll be monitoring. Encrypted channel only. If I text 'bell,' you abort. Get out. Don't finish the interview, don't grab your bag, don't say goodbye. Just leave."
"Bell."
"Bell."

Maya drove south on 101 in the gray October morning, the highway still half-asleep, traffic light enough that she could think. This was the first time she'd been alone since the investigation began — truly alone, no Chloe in the next room, no Lucy's Morse code filling the silence, no hospital monitors or screenshots or S-1 filings. Just the engine and the road and the dull white sky.
She thought about Lucy.

Not the Lucy in the hospital bed. Not the Lucy who'd disappeared into Aeterna's clinical maze. The Lucy before all of it. The Lucy in their shared dorm room, leaning over Maya's desk with a kohl pencil, tilting Maya's chin up with one finger and saying, "Close your eyes, trust me," and drawing a wingtip so sharp it could cut glass. The Lucy who'd shown up at Maya's door at 2 AM the night her father died, not with words — there were never words for that — but with a bag of tamales from the 24-hour place on Mission and a silence so complete it held Maya like water.
The Lucy she'd stopped calling back.

It had seemed reasonable at the time. Lucy's biohacking obsession, her supplement stacks, her clinical-trial rabbit holes — it had all felt like a distraction from the real work of grief. Maya had been trying to hold her life together with rent and shifts and the slow machinery of moving on, and Lucy had been talking about cellular senescence and blood plasma and things that sounded like science fiction. Maya had let the calls go to voicemail. Had texted back shorter and shorter. Had told herself it was self-care, boundaries, the adult thing to do.
The adult thing to do.
Lucy had been trying to save herself, and Maya had decided it was too annoying to listen.
"I'm coming, Luce." The words fogged the windshield for half a second before the defrost ate them.

The Aeterna campus rose from the Palo Alto flatlands like a mirage made permanent. Glass and steel, angles that suggested forward motion, a fountain in the central courtyard that caught the weak morning light and turned it into something almost holy. The landscaping was drought-tolerant and immaculate — succulents, ornamental grasses, the kind of vegetation that looked like it didn't need water because it had transcended the need. Appropriate, for a company selling transcendence.

Maya parked in the visitor lot. She checked her reflection in the rearview mirror — turtleneck straight, hair neat, the sunglasses folded on the passenger seat waiting for her. She put them on. The world went amber. She checked again. Professional. Unremarkable. A woman arriving for a job interview, nothing more.

She walked toward the main entrance on a path of polished concrete, the fountain's mist catching in her peripheral vision. The building's facade was floor-to-ceiling glass, and as she approached, she could see her own reflection walking toward her — a dark figure against the bright interior, getting larger with each step. She watched herself arrive.
The automatic glass doors slid open.
She was still eight feet away.

Maya didn't break stride. She didn't look around for a proximity sensor or a motion plate. She walked through the gap and into Aeterna's lobby, and she did not let herself think about how the building had opened for her before she'd earned it.

The lobby was a study in calculated calm. Driftwood mounted on white walls. Succulents in matte ceramic pots arranged with the precision of a museum exhibit. Ambient meditation music — something with singing bowls and a synthesizer pad underneath, the kind of soundscape designed to lower your heart rate and open your wallet. The air smelled like jasmine and disinfectant, a sterile floral that was probably some proprietary Aeterna fragrance, engineered to make you feel healthy just by breathing.

There was a reception desk — reclaimed wood, a single monitor, a vase of white orchids — and behind it, a woman in a fitted navy dress who looked up as Maya entered and smiled with the practiced warmth of someone trained to greet people in buildings that did not want them to leave.
"Ms. Reyes?"
"That's me."
"Mr. Webb is expecting you. Please follow me."

Maya followed. The receptionist led her through the lobby at a pace that was calm but not slow, past a wall of patents and awards — glass-framed certificates, rows of them, dozens, hundreds, each one a monument to a discovery whose cost would never appear on any filing. The singing bowls faded as they moved from the lobby into a corridor, and the lighting changed without transition — warm amber dropping away to clinical white, the color of hospital hallways, the color of laboratory clean rooms. The floor shifted from polished concrete to something softer, sound-absorbing, and Maya's footsteps went silent.

Her sunglasses camera was recording. She didn't look at the security cameras mounted in the ceiling, but she clocked them — three in the corridor, one at each intersection, the same model, the same blind spot where the wall met the ceiling.

"Mr. Webb's office is just through here," the receptionist said. She paused and turned, and the smile changed — not warmer, but more specific, more personal, like she was about to share a confidence. "He's asked me to let you know that Mr. Vlad may join the interview briefly."

The name landed in Maya's chest like a stone dropped in still water. The ripples spread outward through her ribs, her shoulders, her jaw. She kept her expression neutral. She was an audio engineer. She'd spent years in studios where the red light meant record and one flinch meant a ruined take. She knew how to hold still when everything inside her was screaming.
"Of course," she said. "I'd welcome the opportunity."

The receptionist nodded and turned back toward the door at the end of the corridor. A frosted glass panel, no nameplate, a single brass handle. The meditation music was gone now. The corridor was quiet enough that Maya could hear the high, thin hum of the fluorescent lights above, the subtle ventilation breath of the building's climate control, and underneath it all, the sound of her own pulse in her ears.

Her fingers found the headphones around her neck — over-ear, matte black, professional grade, her daily carry since she was nineteen. She gripped them. The padded band pressed into her palm, familiar and solid, a weight she knew. An anchor in deep water.
The receptionist gestured toward the door and stepped aside.
Mr. Vlad may join the interview briefly.
The monster was in the building.

Maya reached for the handle. The brass was cold under her fingers — colder than it should have been, colder than the corridor's climate could account for, as if the metal itself had been waiting for her touch and had drawn the warmth from her skin on contact. Her thumb found the latch mechanism. She could feel every ridge, every seam, every millimeter of engineered precision in the hardware against her fingertips.
She pressed down.
The latch clicked.
